items.question_text: A controller observes intermittent radar presentation that is inadequate for providing separation services but might still be usable for basic traffic advisories. What does the paragraph require?
items.explanation: The personal satisfaction requirement is tied to the service being provided. If the presentation is inadequate for a specific service, the controller must not provide that service. Adequacy is assessed relative to the service intended — a presentation may be adequate for one service but not another.

choices.text: The controller may provide traffic advisories if satisfied the presentation is adequate for that service, but must withhold separation services if the presentation is inadequate for that purpose.
The controller may provide any radar service as long as the system is powered on.
The controller must stop all radar use entirely regardless of the specific service being considered.
The controller should ask a supervisor to decide which services are permitted.

items.question_text: A radar outage occurs at your facility. OS/CIC notification is mandatory. When must adjacent facilities also be advised?
items.explanation: The paragraph makes OS/CIC notification mandatory ('notify the OS/CIC of any radar malfunctions or unexpected outages') while adjacent-facility notification is conditional ('advise adjacent facilities when appropriate'). The appropriate trigger is when the malfunction or outage could affect the adjacent facility's operations or traffic handoff.

choices.text: Only when the outage extends beyond the facility's own delegated airspace.
When appropriate — if the malfunction could affect the adjacent facility's operations.
In every case, simultaneously with the OS/CIC notification.
Only when the adjacent facility calls to ask about the outage.

items.question_text: A controller applies merging target procedures. Two turbojet aircraft in RVSM airspace are vertically separated by 2,000 feet and their targets appear likely to merge. Must traffic information be issued under merging target procedures?
items.explanation: Traffic information under merging target procedures must be issued when targets appear likely to merge UNLESS the aircraft are separated by more than the appropriate vertical separation minima. Since 2,000 feet exceeds the 1,000-foot RVSM vertical minimum, traffic information is not required by this paragraph.

choices.text: Yes — all turbojet aircraft must receive traffic information regardless of separation.
Yes — 2,000 feet is the minimum vertical separation in RVSM airspace.
No — the aircraft are separated by more than the appropriate vertical separation minima.
No — merging target procedures only apply to aircraft below 10,000 feet.

items.question_text: A controller is unable to provide vector service to an aircraft subject to merging target procedures after the pilot requested a vector to avoid merging. What must the controller do?
items.explanation: The paragraph requires that if unable to provide vector service, the controller must inform the pilot. This ensures the pilot knows not to rely on a vector for separation from the merging traffic and can take other action if needed.

choices.text: Hand off the aircraft to another controller who can provide vectors.
Issue an altitude change and not mention the inability to vector.
Take no action — vector service is always optional under merging target procedures.
Inform the pilot that vector service cannot be provided.

items.question_text: When does the controller's obligation to provide radar surveillance of outer fix holding pattern airspace begin?
items.explanation: The paragraph states surveillance must be provided 'whenever aircraft are holding there.' The triggering condition is the presence of aircraft holding in the outer fix airspace, regardless of time of day, weather conditions, or pilot request.

choices.text: Whenever aircraft are holding in the outer fix holding pattern airspace.
Only when weather is below VFR minimums.
Only when a pilot requests surveillance while holding.
Only during published holding periods in the facility directive.

items.question_text: An RNAV ATS route has 4 miles of protected airspace on each side of the centerline. An aircraft is observed 3 miles left of centerline on a track parallel to the route. Is a deviation advisory required?
items.explanation: The protected airspace extends 4 miles on each side of centerline. At 3 miles off centerline and on a track parallel to the route, the aircraft remains within protected airspace. The deviation advisory is triggered when an aircraft is 'observed in a position and on a track which will obviously cause the aircraft to deviate from its protected airspace area.' At 3 miles with a parallel track, deviation from protected airspace is not obvious. However, the note also indicates aircraft should remain within 2 miles of centerline — exceeding this may indicate a navigation issue worth monitoring.

choices.text: Required because the aircraft exceeds the 2-mile navigation performance standard.
Not yet required — the aircraft remains within the 4-mile protected airspace and its track is parallel, but the controller should monitor for any track change.
Required immediately — any position off the centerline triggers a deviation advisory.
Not required — deviation advisories apply only to non-RNAV routes.

items.question_text: An aircraft has been in radar contact for 30 minutes and the radar display is functioning normally. The controller wants to verify the aircraft's progress over a fix. May the controller request a position report?
items.explanation: The paragraph states 'If necessary, you may request an aircraft to provide an estimate or report over a specific fix.' While the aircraft discontinues compulsory position reporting after receiving 'radar contact,' the controller retains the authority to request reports when necessary. The 'if necessary' qualifier means the controller should have an operational reason.

choices.text: No — once radar contact is established, the controller may never request a position report.
Yes — but only if the radar display has malfunctioned.
Yes — the controller may request a report when necessary, even with a functioning radar display.
No — position reports can only be obtained from non-radar identified aircraft.

items.question_text: An aircraft is equipped with ADS-B Out but its transponder is inoperative. May the controller assign a beacon code to this aircraft?
items.explanation: The paragraph states beacon code assignments must be made to 'only ADS-B and/or transponder-equipped aircraft.' The note clarifies that ADS-B equipped aircraft are also still required to have an operable transponder, and the ATC-assigned beacon code is one of the required message elements of ADS-B Out. Without an operable transponder, the aircraft cannot receive or transmit a beacon code assignment.

choices.text: No — beacon code assignments require an operable transponder; ADS-B alone does not satisfy the requirement.
No — but the aircraft may still be provided radar services without a code.
Yes — ADS-B Out equipment is sufficient to receive a beacon code assignment.
Yes — but only if the aircraft is in Class A airspace.

items.question_text: A controller instructs an aircraft to go to standby 15 miles from its destination. The aircraft enters a multi-target area where clutter is a problem but is only 10 miles from the airport. May the controller use the standby instruction for clutter reduction at this point?
items.explanation: The paragraph provides two independent grounds for the standby instruction: (a) approximately 15 miles from destination when the controller no longer desires transponder/ADS-B operation, OR (b) when necessary to reduce clutter in a multi-target area. Condition (b) does not have a distance requirement, but the controller must instruct the pilot to return to normal position as soon as possible.

choices.text: No — standby for clutter reduction requires the aircraft to be at least 30 miles from the antenna.
Yes — standby may be used to reduce clutter regardless of distance, provided the pilot is instructed to return to normal as soon as possible.
No — standby for clutter is only available when the aircraft is more than 15 miles from destination.
Yes — but only with prior supervisor approval.

items.question_text: During a period of heavy VFR traffic, the controller's display is saturated with VFR target presentations that are interfering with IFR separation. What action may the controller take regarding Code 1200, 1202, 1203, 1255, and 1277 monitoring?
items.explanation: The paragraph permits temporary discontinuation of VFR code monitoring 'during periods when excessive VFR target presentations derogate the separation of IFR traffic.' This is a workload-relief provision for when VFR targets overwhelm the display and threaten IFR separation quality.

choices.text: Permanently filter those codes from the display for the remainder of the shift.
Continue monitoring — VFR code monitoring may never be interrupted.
Temporarily discontinue monitoring of those VFR codes until the excessive presentation subsides.
Discontinue IFR separation services until the VFR traffic clears.

items.question_text: An aircraft's transponder appears to be malfunctioning. After informing the pilot and issuing reset instructions, what additional notification is required?
items.explanation: The paragraph requires that the subsequent control position in the facility or the next facility, as applicable, be notified when an aircraft transponder is malfunctioning or inoperative. This ensures downstream controllers are aware of the equipment limitation before the aircraft enters their airspace.

choices.text: Notify the OS/CIC only.
Only the pilot needs to be informed — no further notification is required.
Notify all aircraft on the frequency of the malfunctioning transponder.
Notify the subsequent control position in the facility or the next facility, as applicable.

items.question_text: A controller has previously approved an aircraft to operate with a failed transponder. Traffic conditions have since become heavy. On what basis may the controller withdraw the approval?
items.explanation: The paragraph states the controller may disapprove a request or withdraw a previously issued approval 'solely on the basis of traffic conditions or other operational factors.' No other justification is needed — operational factors alone are sufficient grounds.

choices.text: Solely on the basis of traffic conditions or other operational factors.
Only if the aircraft's transponder failure is confirmed by maintenance personnel.
Only with the pilot's concurrence.
Only if the aircraft has entered a different facility's airspace.

items.question_text: A controller accepts an interfacility handoff from another ERAM facility. The Mode C readout shows no unusual indicators. Is Mode C validation required?
items.explanation: ERAM facilities are normally not required to validate Mode C altitude readouts after accepting interfacility handoffs from other ERAM facilities. However, this exemption does not apply after initial track start or track start from coast is required, or during and after the display of a missing, unreasonable, exceptional, or otherwise unreliable Mode C readout indicator. Since the scenario describes no unusual indicators and it is a routine ERAM-to-ERAM handoff, validation is not required.

choices.text: Yes — validation is required after every interfacility handoff without exception.
No — ERAM facilities are not required to validate Mode C after accepting interfacility handoffs from other ERAM facilities, absent an unreliable readout indicator.
Yes — validation is required unless the handoff is from a CTRD-equipped tower.
No — validation is never required for interfacility handoffs.

items.question_text: An aircraft makes initial contact with a sector. The pilot's transmission includes only a callsign. The Mode C readout is valid and shows the aircraft level at its assigned altitude. No new altitude has been assigned. What is the controller's obligation regarding altitude confirmation?
items.explanation: Altitude confirmation on initial contact is required UNLESS one of the exceptions applies. Exception (c) states confirmation is not required when 'the Mode C readout is valid and indicates that the aircraft is established at the assigned altitude.' Since both conditions are met, altitude confirmation is not required.

choices.text: No — but only because no new altitude was assigned.
Yes — altitude confirmation is always required on initial contact regardless of Mode C.
No — a valid Mode C readout showing the aircraft established at the assigned altitude is an exception to the confirmation requirement.
Yes — the pilot must verbally state the altitude on every initial contact.

items.question_text: For non-Mode C aircraft, which of the following would still require altitude confirmation on initial contact? (All others are exceptions.)
items.explanation: For non-Mode C aircraft, the exceptions are: (1) the pilot states the assigned altitude, (2) a new altitude is assigned to a climbing or descending aircraft, or (3) TERMINAL — the aircraft was transferred from another sector/position within the same facility (intrafacility). A valid Mode C readout is NOT an exception for non-Mode C aircraft because, by definition, these aircraft lack Mode C. The USA-only reconfirmation requirement for all pilot altitude readbacks is an additional obligation, not an exception.

choices.text: TERMINAL — the aircraft was transferred from another sector/position within the facility.
The pilot states the assigned altitude on initial contact.
A new altitude is assigned to a climbing or descending aircraft.
A valid Mode C readout showing the aircraft at the assigned altitude.

items.question_text: An aircraft that was never equipped with a transponder requests an inflight deviation from the Mode C transponder requirement during a routine flight. What is the controller's required response?
items.explanation: Paragraph 5-2-19a states: 'Except in an emergency, do not approve inflight requests for authorization to deviate from 14 CFR section 91.215(b)(5)(i) requirements originated by aircraft without transponder equipment installed.' Aircraft that never had a transponder installed are ineligible for deviation approval outside of an emergency.

choices.text: Do not approve the request — deviation approval for aircraft without transponder equipment installed is only permitted in an emergency.
Approve the request if traffic conditions permit.
Approve the request if the aircraft provides a VFR route of flight and maintains radio contact.
Suggest the aircraft file an IFR flight plan, then approve the deviation.

items.question_text: During a handoff, the transferring controller coordinates that the aircraft may change its beacon code before entering the receiving controller's airspace. The receiving controller agrees. Under what authority may the code change occur before the boundary crossing?
items.explanation: The paragraph prohibits requesting a code change before the aircraft is within your area of responsibility UNLESS 'otherwise specified in a directive or a letter of agreement or coordinated at the time of handoff.' Since the controllers coordinated at the time of handoff, the code change is permitted.

choices.text: The code change is permitted only by the transferring controller, not the receiving controller.
The code change is permitted because it was coordinated at the time of handoff, which is an explicit exception.
The code change is not permitted — the aircraft must be within the receiving controller's airspace first.
The code change is permitted only if specified in a letter of agreement — verbal coordination is insufficient.

items.question_text: A terminal controller is responsible for airspace up to 10,000 feet. What is the minimum acceptable upper altitude filter limit for Mode C readout display?
items.explanation: The paragraph requires setting the upper limits 'no lower than 1,000 feet above the highest altitude for which the controller is responsible.' With responsibility up to 10,000 feet, the upper filter must be set to at least 11,000 feet.

choices.text: 10,000 feet — matching the highest altitude of responsibility.
12,500 feet — at least 2,500 feet above for safety margin.
11,000 feet — at least 1,000 feet above the highest altitude of responsibility.
No specific minimum — the controller may set filters as needed.

items.question_text: A controller observes that an aircraft's ADS-B transmitter appears inoperative. Which information must be included in the OS/CIC notification?
items.explanation: The paragraph specifies that the OS/CIC must be notified of the 'aircraft call sign, location, and time of the occurrence (UTC).' All three elements are required.

choices.text: Location and time only — the call sign is in the system.
Aircraft call sign and location only — time is recorded automatically.
Aircraft call sign only — the OS/CIC can determine the rest.
Aircraft call sign, location, and time of the occurrence in UTC.

items.question_text: A pilot requests to turn off their ADS-B Out transmitter during a flight. The aircraft has functional ADS-B Out avionics installed. What is the controller's proper response?
items.explanation: The paragraph prohibits approving any pilot request for ADS-B Out OFF operations. The controller must notify the OS/CIC of the request including aircraft call sign and location. Authorization for ADS-B Out OFF operations must come from FAA System Operations Security, not from ATC.

choices.text: Do not approve the request and notify the OS/CIC of the request including aircraft call sign and location.
Instruct the pilot to turn off ADS-B Out and squawk 1200.
Approve the request if traffic conditions are light.
Approve the request since the pilot has operational need.

items.question_text: A controller observes Code 7600 displayed in an aircraft's data block. The ERAM system displays 'RDOF.' What is the controller's immediate procedural obligation?
items.explanation: The paragraph directs the controller to 'apply the procedures in paragraph 10-4-4, Communications Failure' when Code 7600 (or its ERAM equivalent 'RDOF') is observed. This is a cross-reference directive — the controller must know and apply the communications failure procedures in the referenced paragraph.

choices.text: Attempt to reestablish communications on the emergency frequency.
Apply the communications failure procedures in paragraph 10-4-4.
Squawk 7600 for the aircraft.
No action is required — 7600 is informational only.

items.question_text: A controller observes 'HIJK' displayed in the ERAM data block instead of the numeric code 7500. What does this display indicate and what action is required?
items.explanation: The note states that ERAM displays 'HIJK' instead of '7500' in the data block. When this display is observed, the controller must apply the procedures in paragraph 10-2-6, Hijacked Aircraft. The alphanumeric display replaces the numeric code for quicker recognition.

choices.text: This is a system error; no pilot action is required.
The aircraft has a communications failure; apply paragraph 10-4-4 procedures.
The aircraft is squawking Code 7500 for hijack/unlawful interference; apply paragraph 10-2-6 procedures.
The aircraft has declared an emergency; issue Code 7700.

items.question_text: A VFR aircraft receiving radar advisories is outside the controller's area of responsibility but an operational benefit exists in retaining the aircraft on frequency. What must occur before issuing a control instruction?
items.explanation: When a VFR aircraft receiving radar advisories is outside the controller's area of responsibility, coordination must be effected (a) as soon as possible after positive identification and (b) prior to issuing a control instruction or providing a service other than a safety alert/traffic advisory. Safety alerts and traffic advisories are exceptions that may be issued before coordination.

choices.text: No coordination is needed if the aircraft is already on the frequency.
Coordination is only needed if the aircraft will enter Class B airspace.
Coordination must be completed within 5 minutes after the control instruction is issued.
Coordination must be effected before issuing any control instruction or service other than a safety alert or traffic advisory.

items.question_text: A pressure suit flight is assigned one of the special use Mode 3/A codes reserved under FAA Order JO 7610.4. The aircraft requests a code change for operational reasons. What must the controller ensure?
items.explanation: The paragraph requires that these flights 'remain on one of the specially designated beacon codes unless otherwise coordinated.' The default rule is to keep the aircraft on the special use code; any deviation requires explicit coordination.

choices.text: Keep the aircraft on one of the specially designated codes unless otherwise coordinated.
Assign any available discrete code since all codes are interchangeable.
Assign Code 1200 since the aircraft is operating under special procedures.
Allow the pilot to select any code they prefer.

items.question_text: Before providing radar service, what must the controller establish and maintain, and what are the exceptions?
items.explanation: The paragraph requires establishing and maintaining radar identification before providing radar service, with exceptions in paragraph 5-5-1 (certain subparagraphs) and paragraph 8-5-5. This is a foundational prerequisite — radar service cannot be provided to unidentified targets except where specifically excepted.

choices.text: Issue a beacon code — no further identification is required.
Establish and maintain radar identification of the aircraft involved, except as provided in specific paragraphs.
Establish radio communications only — radar identification is optional.
Confirm the aircraft's altitude — identification follows automatically.

items.question_text: A controller observes a departing aircraft target within 1 mile of the takeoff runway end at an airport with an operating control tower. What must be accomplished in addition to this observation for the departing aircraft identification method to be valid?
items.explanation: The departing aircraft identification method requires the target to be within 1 mile of the takeoff runway end at an airport with an operating control tower AND one of several specified coordination methods must be accomplished. Without any of those coordination methods, the observation alone is insufficient for identification.

choices.text: Wait until the aircraft reaches 3 miles from the airport before considering it identified.
No additional step is needed — visual observation of the target within 1 mile of the runway end is sufficient alone.
Accomplish one of the specified coordination methods.
Request the pilot to activate the IDENT feature on the transponder.

items.question_text: A terminal controller sees an auto-acquired aircraft data block displayed and visible. The aircraft was previously identified by the transferring controller using beacon identification procedures and the data block was handed off. Is the aircraft considered identified?
items.explanation: The paragraph lists conditions for considering an auto-acquired aircraft as identified. Condition 1 is that 'the radar or beacon identification procedures have been used to confirm the identity of the tagged target.' If the transferring controller already confirmed identity using proper procedures and handed off the tag, this condition is met, and the auto-acquired aircraft with a visible data block is considered identified.

choices.text: No — auto-acquired aircraft must always be re-identified by the receiving controller.
No — auto-acquisition is for tracking only and does not constitute identification.
Yes — but only if the aircraft has also made an identifying turn.
Yes — the data block is displayed and visible, and the tagged target's identity was previously confirmed through identification procedures.

items.question_text: Two aircraft targets are in close proximity on the radar display and the controller cannot be certain which target corresponds to which aircraft. What is the required action?
items.explanation: The paragraph requires using more than one method of identification when proximity of targets, duplication of observed action, or any other circumstances cause doubt as to target identification. A single identification method is insufficient when doubt exists.

choices.text: Use more than one method of identification to resolve the doubt.
Ask the pilots which target they think they are.
Use the closest target to the expected position as the identified aircraft.
Continue with one identification method — proximity alone does not justify using multiple methods.

items.question_text: A controller establishes initial radar identification of an aircraft using an identifying turn. Is position information required, and if so, when must it be provided?
items.explanation: The paragraph requires informing an aircraft of its position whenever radar identification is established by means of identifying turns or by any of the beacon identification methods in paragraph 5-3-3. Position information is not required when identification is established by other methods such as the departing aircraft method or handoff.

choices.text: Position information is required only when the pilot requests it.
Position information must be given because identification was established by an identifying turn.
Position information is not required for any identification method.
Position information is required only for beacon identification methods, not for identifying turns.

items.question_text: An aircraft was previously in radar contact, then radar service was terminated. The same controller later reestablishes radar identification with the same aircraft. What must the controller tell the aircraft?
items.explanation: The paragraph requires informing an aircraft of radar contact when radar identification is reestablished subsequent to loss of radar contact or terminating radar service. Even though the aircraft was previously identified, the reestablishment requires the 'RADAR CONTACT' notification.

choices.text: No notification is needed because the aircraft was previously identified.
Inform the aircraft of radar service termination before reestablishing contact.
Inform the aircraft of radar contact because identification was reestablished after termination of radar service.
Only inform the aircraft if it requests confirmation of radar contact.

items.question_text: What is the operational purpose of transferring radar identification from one controller to another?
items.explanation: The paragraph states the purpose is to 'provide continuous radar service to an aircraft and facilitate a safe, orderly, and expeditious flow of traffic.' Identification transfer is a mechanism for service continuity, not merely an administrative procedure.

choices.text: To satisfy automation system requirements for data block display.
To reduce the workload of the transferring controller.
To comply with facility directive requirements for shift changes.
To provide continuous radar service and facilitate a safe, orderly, and expeditious flow of traffic.

items.question_text: How does a handoff differ from a point-out in terms of radio communications?
items.explanation: A handoff involves transfer of radar identification where the aircraft will enter the receiving controller's airspace AND radio communications will be transferred. A point-out is an action taken to transfer radar identification where the aircraft will enter the receiving controller's airspace but radio communications will NOT be transferred. The key distinction is whether communications transfer occurs.

choices.text: In a handoff, radio communications transfer to the receiving controller; in a point-out, communications remain with the transferring controller.
A point-out requires the aircraft to enter the receiving controller's airspace; a handoff does not.
There is no difference — the terms are interchangeable.
A handoff transfers only the data block; a point-out transfers only the radio frequency.

items.question_text: A transferring controller needs to transfer radar identification, but the receiving controller's system lacks automated handoff capability. What must the transferring controller do to complete a valid transfer?
items.explanation: The paragraph requires transferring radar identification by 'at least one' of the listed methods. If automation is not available at the receiving end, the transfer is not effectively accomplished. Another method (physical pointing or landline voice) must be used. The note states automated handoff capabilities are a function of the automation software at the receiving controller's position.

choices.text: No — handoffs are impossible without automated capabilities on both sides.
No — the transfer must be accomplished by a method available to both controllers; use physical pointing or landline voice instead.
Yes — automated transfer from the sending side is sufficient regardless of receiving capability.
Yes — but the receiving controller must acknowledge within 30 seconds.

items.question_text: A controller uses the term 'traffic' to coordinate separation with another controller. What obligation does each controller assume?
items.explanation: The paragraph establishes a two-sided obligation: the controller issuing traffic must issue appropriate restrictions, and the controller accepting the restrictions must be responsible to ensure that approved separation is maintained between the involved aircraft. Both controllers have defined responsibilities.

choices.text: Only the issuing controller has any responsibility for separation.
Only the accepting controller has any responsibility for separation.
The issuing controller must provide appropriate restrictions; the accepting controller is responsible to ensure approved separation is maintained.
Neither controller has specific responsibility — separation is automated.

items.question_text: An en route controller initiates an automated point-out to a receiving controller. The receiving controller takes no action. What must the transferring controller do?
items.explanation: The paragraph states that for en route, automated approval may be utilized in lieu of verbal approval, but if the receiving controller takes no action, the transferring controller must revert to verbal coordination. This is a safety backstop — silence is not consent.

choices.text: Wait indefinitely for the receiving controller to respond.
Revert to verbal coordination to obtain approval.
Assume approval and permit the aircraft to enter the receiving controller's airspace.
Cancel the point-out and vector the aircraft away from the boundary.

items.question_text: Unless otherwise coordinated or specified in an LOA or facility directive, when must the transferring controller complete a handoff?
items.explanation: The transferring controller must complete a handoff prior to an aircraft entering the airspace delegated to the receiving controller. This is a timing requirement — the handoff must be completed before the boundary crossing, not after.

choices.text: Within 2 minutes after the aircraft enters the receiving controller's airspace.
At any time, as long as radio communications have been transferred.
Only when the receiving controller requests the handoff.
Prior to the aircraft entering the airspace delegated to the receiving controller.

items.question_text: Before accepting a handoff, what must the receiving controller verify about the target and data block?
items.explanation: The receiving controller must ensure that the target position corresponds with the position given by the transferring controller OR that there is an appropriate association between an automated data block and the target being transferred. This is a verification step before acceptance to prevent misidentification.

choices.text: That the target position corresponds with the given position or that there is appropriate association between the data block and target.
That the aircraft's altitude matches the flight plan exactly.
That the transferring controller has issued approach clearance.
That the aircraft is squawking the correct code — position verification is optional.

items.question_text: Under what three conditions may radar identification be transferred without verbal coordination using AIT?
items.explanation: The paragraph lists three required conditions, connected by 'and': (a) during radar handoff; AND (b) via information displayed in full data blocks; AND (c) when following procedures specified in the facility AIT directive and/or LOA. All three must be satisfied for silent transfer.

choices.text: Only during shift changes when the same controller position is involved.
During any handoff, with any data block display, at any time.
During radar handoff, via full data blocks, and when following facility AIT directive or LOA procedures.
When the transferring controller decides verbal coordination is unnecessary.

items.question_text: To which aircraft must radar separation be applied according to the application paragraph?
items.explanation: The paragraph specifically requires radar separation for 'all RNAV aircraft operating at and below FL450 on Q routes or random RNAV routes, excluding oceanic airspace.' There is also an exception for point-to-point routes via an impromptu route. This does not mean radar separation is limited to these aircraft — other separation rules apply elsewhere — but this paragraph mandates radar separation for this specific category.

choices.text: All aircraft regardless of navigation type or altitude.
Only turbojet aircraft at any altitude on any route.
Only non-RNAV aircraft on conventional airways.
All RNAV aircraft operating at and below FL450 on Q routes or random RNAV routes, excluding oceanic airspace.

items.question_text: An aircraft is 4 miles from the boundary of adjacent airspace where radar separation is also being used. In the absence of coordination between the controllers, what is the correct assessment of whether this separation meets the boundary minimum?
items.explanation: The adjacent airspace boundary separation minima depend on distance from the antenna. When less than 40 miles from the antenna, the minimum is 3 miles; when 40 miles or more, it is 5 miles. Since the aircraft is already at 4 miles, this meets the 3-mile minimum if within 40 miles of the antenna.

choices.text: The aircraft meets the 3-mile minimum if less than 40 miles from the antenna, but does not meet the 5-mile minimum if 40 miles or more from the antenna.
Coordination must be effected before any separation minimum can be applied.
4 miles is always sufficient regardless of antenna distance.
The minimum is always 5 miles from any airspace boundary.

items.question_text: When using a radar display with a previously specified 1/2 mile beacon target displacement to separate a beacon target from a primary target, what separation must be applied?
items.explanation: The paragraph requires adding a 1-mile correction factor to the applicable minima when using a display with a previously specified beacon target displacement. If the applicable minimum would normally be 3 miles, the controller must apply 4 miles (3 + 1). The maximum allowable displacement for applying this correction is 1 mile.

choices.text: Double the applicable minimum.
The applicable minimum with no correction needed.
The applicable minimum plus a 1-mile correction factor.
A fixed 5 miles regardless of the applicable minimum.

items.question_text: When applying radar separation between primary radar targets, what condition must be avoided?
items.explanation: The paragraph states separation is applied between the centers of primary radar targets but the controller must NOT allow a primary target to touch another primary target or a beacon control slash. Touching targets indicate a loss of separation.

choices.text: A primary target touching a data block.
Two beacon control slashes pointing toward each other.
An aircraft target touching the scope boundary.
A primary target touching another primary target or a beacon control slash.

items.question_text: A controller applies target resolution procedures. What must be issued when this procedure is used?
items.explanation: The paragraph states that mandatory traffic advisories and safety alerts must be issued when target resolution procedures are used. Additionally, the procedure must not be provided utilizing mosaic radar systems. Target resolution is a process to ensure correlated or digitized targets do not touch.

choices.text: Mandatory traffic advisories and safety alerts.
A frequency change to the final controller.
An altitude assignment change for both aircraft.
Nothing additional — target resolution is a display function only.

items.question_text: When aircraft lack lateral separation, what condition must be met for altitude assignment to provide valid vertical separation?
items.explanation: The paragraph requires that valid Mode C altitude information must be monitored and the applicable separation minima must be maintained at all times. Without valid Mode C monitoring, altitude assignments alone do not satisfy vertical separation requirements.

choices.text: Only non-Mode C aircraft can be vertically separated by altitude assignment.
Valid Mode C altitude information must be monitored and applicable separation minima maintained at all times.
The pilot must verbally confirm each altitude assignment within 30 seconds.
No additional conditions — altitude assignment is always sufficient for vertical separation.

items.question_text: Why is additional separation required for formation flights beyond standard minima?
items.explanation: The paragraph explains that additional separation is necessary because of the distance allowed between formation aircraft and the lead aircraft. Standard separation applied to the lead aircraft does not account for the periphery of the formation, which may extend significantly beyond the lead aircraft's position.

choices.text: Because the distance allowed between formation aircraft and the lead aircraft means the formation's periphery extends beyond the lead's position.
Because formation aircraft fly at higher speeds than individual aircraft.
Because formation flights always operate in Class A airspace.
Because formation flights are not subject to standard separation rules.

items.question_text: When vectoring an aircraft, the controller specifies a magnetic heading and a direction of turn. What is the prescribed phraseology format?
items.explanation: The prescribed phraseology is 'TURN LEFT/RIGHT HEADING (degrees)' when both direction and heading are specified. This ensures unambiguous turn instructions.

choices.text: TURN TO HEADING (degrees) LEFT/RIGHT.
TURN LEFT/RIGHT HEADING (degrees).
HEADING (degrees) WITH LEFT/RIGHT TURN.
MAKE A LEFT/RIGHT TURN TO (degrees).

items.question_text: A controller needs an aircraft to reduce speed by 20 knots. Which instruction format is correct for a speed reduction by a specified number of knots?
items.explanation: The paragraph specifies that speed adjustments can be in single-digit form (a specific speed) or in group form (a specified number of knots). The phraseology for the group form is 'REDUCE SPEED (number) KNOTS.' This tests the distinction between assigning a specific speed value vs. a reduction amount.

choices.text: SLOW TO TWO ZERO KNOTS LESS THAN PRESENT SPEED.
REDUCE SPEED BY TWO ZERO.
REDUCE SPEED TWO ZERO KNOTS.
DECREASE SPEED TWO ZERO KNOTS.

items.question_text: A controller is vectoring a departing aircraft on a radar SID. May the controller concurrently use a diverse vector area (DVA) for the same departure?
items.explanation: The paragraph explicitly prohibits concurrent use of a DVA when vectoring a departing aircraft on a radar SID. These are mutually exclusive procedures — the controller must use one or the other, not both simultaneously.

choices.text: Yes — DVA and SID can be used together for added safety margin.
No — concurrent use of a DVA is not permitted when vectoring on a radar SID.
Yes — but only when the reported ceiling is below 1,000 feet.
Yes — but only with approach control approval.

items.question_text: At a location that has a diverse vector area (DVA), can the controller use both a SID and the DVA simultaneously when assigning an initial heading?
items.explanation: The paragraph states that at locations that have a DVA, concurrent use of both a SID and DVA is not permitted. The initial heading must be consistent with either the SID OR the DVA, not both.

choices.text: Yes — DVA may be used as a backup to SID headings.
Yes — but only for turbojet aircraft.
No — concurrent use of both a SID and DVA is not permitted.
Yes — DVA and SID headings can be combined for flexibility.

items.question_text: A controller is vectoring an aircraft to intercept the final approach course. The reported ceiling is 500 feet above the MVA and visibility is unrestricted. At what minimum distance from the approach gate must the intercept occur?
items.explanation: The default minimum is at least 2 miles outside the approach gate. There are exceptions: when the reported ceiling is at least 500 feet above the MVA/MIA and visibility is at least 3 miles, the intercept may be at a distance closer than 2 miles. But 'at least 2 miles' is the standard, and the exceptions permit intercept at less than 2 miles — they don't prohibit it at 2 miles.

choices.text: At least 2 miles — this is the standard minimum regardless of weather conditions.
Less than 2 miles is permitted because the ceiling is at least 500 feet above the MVA and visibility is unrestricted.
At least 3 miles — the weather exception only reduces the requirement when conditions are below the ceiling threshold.
At the approach gate — weather conditions eliminate the need for any buffer.

items.question_text: What is the prerequisite for conducting simultaneous independent approaches to widely-spaced parallel runways without final monitors?
items.explanation: The paragraph states that simultaneous independent approaches to widely-spaced parallel runways 'may only be conducted where instrument approach charts specifically authorize simultaneous approaches.' Chart authorization is a non-negotiable prerequisite.

choices.text: A facility directive may authorize such approaches even without chart authorization.
The controller may authorize based on weather conditions alone.
Instrument approach charts must specifically authorize simultaneous approaches.
Any parallel runways separated by at least 2,500 feet qualify automatically.

items.question_text: A controller vectors an aircraft across the final approach course but is unable to inform the aircraft. According to the note, what is the pilot expected to do?
items.explanation: The note states that if the controller is unable to inform the aircraft of the cross-final vector, 'the pilot is not expected to turn inbound on the final approach course unless approach clearance has been issued.' Without approach clearance and without the cross-final advisory, the pilot should continue the assigned heading.

choices.text: Continue the assigned heading and not turn inbound on the final approach course unless approach clearance has been issued.
Turn inbound on the final approach course when the navigation indicator centers.
Contact the controller to request approach clearance immediately.
Execute a missed approach since a cross-final vector was not communicated.

items.question_text: Under what conditions may a radar approach be offered to an aircraft?
items.explanation: The paragraph states a radar approach may be given to any aircraft upon request AND may be OFFERED to aircraft in distress regardless of weather conditions or to expedite traffic. The controller has discretion to offer a radar approach in these two circumstances, even without a pilot request.

choices.text: Only when the pilot specifically requests a radar approach.
Only when weather is below VFR minimums.
Only to aircraft that have filed an IFR flight plan.
To aircraft in distress regardless of weather, or to expedite traffic.

items.question_text: Before an aircraft starts final descent for a full-stop landing, weather reports indicate IFR conditions on final approach. What must the controller issue?
items.explanation: The paragraph requires issuing a specific missed approach procedure approved for the radar approach being conducted before the aircraft starts final descent when any portion of the final approach will be in IFR conditions. This ensures the pilot has the missed approach instructions before beginning the critical descent phase.

choices.text: A specific missed approach procedure approved for the radar approach being conducted.
A frequency change to the tower.
The current ATIS code and altimeter setting.
No additional information is required beyond the approach clearance.

items.question_text: How frequently should transmissions with an aircraft on surveillance final approach occur?
items.explanation: The paragraph specifies that transmissions with aircraft on surveillance final approach 'should occur approximately every 15 seconds.' This is a guidance frequency, not an absolute rule, but represents the expected transmission interval for adequate course monitoring.

choices.text: Only when the aircraft deviates from course.
Approximately every 15 seconds.
Approximately every 5 seconds.
Approximately every 30 seconds.

items.question_text: When must the controller inform an aircraft that it is approaching glidepath on a PAR approach?
items.explanation: The paragraph specifies the notification should occur approximately 10 to 30 seconds before final descent. This provides the pilot a brief window to prepare for the descent transition.

choices.text: At 1 mile from the runway threshold.
Exactly at the point where final descent begins.
Approximately 10 to 30 seconds before final descent.
Only when the pilot requests glidepath information.

items.question_text: The elevation portion of PAR equipment fails during a precision approach and the aircraft cannot take over visually. What action must the controller take?
items.explanation: The paragraph states that if the elevation portion fails: (1) discontinue PAR instructions, tell the aircraft to take over visually, or if unable, execute a missed approach. If the aircraft executes a missed approach, apply the no-glidepath missed approach procedure. The controller should also inform the aircraft that no-glidepath guidance is available for a surveillance approach if the ASR is operational.

choices.text: Continue issuing azimuth-only guidance since the course information is still available.
Hand off the aircraft to tower for a visual approach.
Ask the pilot to use their own altimeter for glidepath reference.
Discontinue PAR instructions, tell the aircraft to execute a missed approach, and apply the no-glidepath missed approach procedure.

items.question_text: Under what conditions may PAR approaches be conducted when the ASR is unusable?
items.explanation: The paragraph provides two conditions: (1) a nonradar instrument approach will position the aircraft over a navigational aid or DME fix within the precision radar coverage, OR (2) an adjacent radar facility can provide a direct radar handoff to the PAR controller. Either condition is sufficient.

choices.text: When a nonradar instrument approach will position the aircraft within PAR coverage, or an adjacent radar facility can provide a direct radar handoff to the PAR controller.
Only when the aircraft is equipped with an IFR-certified GPS.
PAR approaches cannot be conducted without a functioning ASR under any circumstances.
Only when tower can provide visual separation for the approach sequence.

items.question_text: An E-MSAW alert activates for an aircraft. What caution does the note provide about the controller's response?
items.explanation: The note cautions that when issuing a clearance to an aircraft in reaction to an E-MSAW alert, care should be taken to ensure that adjacent MIA areas are not a factor. A clearance that resolves the current alert could direct the aircraft into a different MIA area, creating a new hazard.

choices.text: Request the pilot verify their altitude before taking any action.
Issue an immediate climb to FL 600 to clear all terrain.
Ensure that adjacent MIA areas are not a factor before issuing a clearance in reaction to the alert.
Disable the E-MSAW system until the alert can be investigated.

items.question_text: May coast tracks be used for applying radar separation criteria?
items.explanation: The paragraph is unequivocal: 'Do not use coast tracks in the application of either radar or nonradar separation criteria.' Coast tracks are projected positions, not actual radar returns, and cannot serve as a basis for separation.

choices.text: Yes — but only for nonradar separation, not radar.
Yes — but only for radar separation, not nonradar.
Yes — coast tracks are equivalent to actual radar returns for separation purposes.
No — coast tracks must not be used for either radar or nonradar separation criteria.

items.question_text: A controller needs to initiate a coast track as a reminder for an aircraft without a corresponding computer-stored flight plan. In what mode must the track be initiated?
items.explanation: The paragraph requires initiating coast tracks only in Flight Plan Aided Tracking (FLAT) mode, EXCEPT that 'free' coast tracking may be used as a reminder for aircraft without computer-stored flight plan information. Since this aircraft lacks a stored flight plan, 'free' coast tracking is the appropriate exception.

choices.text: Free coast tracking may be used because the aircraft lacks a corresponding computer-stored flight plan.
FLAT mode is required regardless of flight plan status.
Coast tracks cannot be initiated for aircraft without stored flight plans.
Either FLAT or free mode may be used at the controller's discretion.

items.question_text: Which responsibilities remain with the controller regardless of terminal automation equipment assistance?
items.explanation: The paragraph lists four responsibilities that remain with the controller regardless of automation: proper identification, maintenance of identity, handoff of the correct target associated with the alphanumeric data, and separation of aircraft. Automation assists but does not replace the controller's responsibility for these core functions.

choices.text: Data block display management only — all other functions are automated.
Proper identification, maintenance of identity, correct target handoff, and separation of aircraft.
Only separation of aircraft — identification is fully automated.
Only handoff and identification — separation is handled by the system.

items.question_text: A VFR aircraft receiving flight following cancels its IFR flight plan and the pilot makes no request for MSAW processing. What is the controller's required action for MSAW processing?
items.explanation: The paragraph requires inhibiting MSAW processing of VFR aircraft and aircraft that cancel IFR flight plans UNLESS the pilot specifically requests otherwise. Since the pilot did not request MSAW processing after cancellation, MSAW should be inhibited.

choices.text: MSAW processing is automatic and cannot be inhibited by the controller.
Continue MSAW processing — IFR cancellation does not affect MSAW.
Enable MSAW processing as a precaution after any IFR cancellation.
Inhibit MSAW processing unless the pilot specifically requests otherwise.

items.question_text: A controller is using Mode C altitude information for vertical separation. An aircraft is on a cruise clearance at 5,000 feet and another aircraft is assigned 6,000 feet. May the controller rely on Mode C for vertical separation between these aircraft?
items.explanation: The paragraph prohibits using Mode C to effect vertical separation with an aircraft on a cruise clearance, contact approach, or as specified in paragraph 5-14-4 subparagraph f3. Since one aircraft is on a cruise clearance, Mode C cannot be used for vertical separation between these aircraft regardless of the altitude difference.

choices.text: Yes — the aircraft are vertically separated by 1,000 feet and both have valid Mode C readouts.
Yes — the cruise clearance restriction only applies above FL 180.
No — Mode C cannot be used for vertical separation when one aircraft is on a cruise clearance.
No — Mode C vertical separation requires at least 2,000 feet between aircraft.

items.question_text: In terminal single site ASR or FUSION mode, two aircraft are on opposite courses and the controller observes they have passed each other. What type of separation may the controller now apply?
items.explanation: When aircraft are on opposite/reciprocal courses and the controller has observed that they have passed each other, all other approved separation may be discontinued and passing or diverging separation applied. The key operational trigger is the observed passing — once the aircraft have crossed, the collision risk has been resolved.

choices.text: No separation is required after aircraft have passed each other.
Standard 3-mile radar separation must continue to be maintained.
Vertical separation of at least 1,000 feet must be applied in addition to radar separation.
Passing or diverging separation — all other approved separation may be discontinued.

items.question_text: An aircraft climbing through FL 500 is being separated from another aircraft tracked to the edge of the scope. The controller is using EAS. What separation from the edge of scope is required?
items.explanation: Under EAS edge-of-scope separation, aircraft below FL 600 require 5 miles from the edge of scope; at FL 600 and above, 10 miles is required. Since the aircraft is at FL 500 (below FL 600), the applicable minimum is 5 miles. Note this is different from the terminal minima of 3 miles (less than 40 NM from antenna) and 5 miles (40 NM or more).

choices.text: 3 miles — EAS uses the same minima as terminal operations.
5 miles — the EAS minimum for aircraft below FL 600.
10 miles — all EAS edge-of-scope separation requires 10 miles.
5 miles from the antenna, measured differently than from the edge of scope.

items.question_text: A controller assigns a speed to an aircraft operating at FL 250. What is the minimum speed that may be assigned?
items.explanation: For aircraft operating between FL 280 and 10,000 feet, the minimum assignable speed is 250 knots or the equivalent Mach number. FL 250 falls within this altitude band. The note provides Mach equivalents for standard day conditions; at FL 250 it is approximately 0.61 Mach.

choices.text: 200 knots — the standard minimum for all aircraft regardless of altitude.
280 knots — the minimum for aircraft above FL 200.
No minimum applies — the controller may assign any speed needed operationally.
250 knots or the equivalent Mach number (approximately 0.61 at FL 250).

items.question_text: A controller has been applying speed adjustments to an arriving aircraft. The aircraft is now on final approach and ATC-assigned speed adjustments are no longer needed. The published instrument approach procedure includes a speed restriction of 200 knots at the final approach fix. What should the controller tell the pilot?
items.explanation: The phrase 'resume normal speed' is used only when there is no underlying published speed restriction. Since the published procedure includes a 200-knot speed restriction at the final approach fix, 'resume normal speed' is not appropriate. The controller should advise that ATC-assigned speed adjustments are no longer needed, but the pilot must still comply with the published speed restriction.

choices.text: Advise that ATC-assigned speed adjustments are no longer needed, but do not use 'resume normal speed' because a published speed restriction applies.
No transmission is needed — speed adjustments terminate automatically when the aircraft is on final approach.
Issue 'resume normal speed' — this is the standard phraseology for terminating any speed adjustment.
Issue 'cancel speed restrictions' — this removes both ATC-assigned and published restrictions.

items.question_text: A terminal controller is separating a departing aircraft from an arriving aircraft on final approach. The radar antenna is 45 miles away. The departing aircraft will be released when the arriving aircraft is 2 miles from the runway. What is the required separation outcome?
items.explanation: The departure-arrival separation minimum is 2 miles, provided separation will increase to a minimum of 3 miles (or 5 miles when 40 miles or more from the antenna) within 1 minute after takeoff. At 45 miles from the antenna, the increasing-to minimum is 5 miles. So the separation must increase from 2 miles to at least 5 miles within 1 minute of takeoff.

choices.text: Separation must increase from 3 miles to 5 miles within 1 minute after takeoff.
Separation must increase from 2 miles to at least 3 miles within 1 minute after takeoff.
The departing aircraft cannot be released — 3 miles of separation is required before takeoff.
Separation must increase from 2 miles to at least 5 miles within 1 minute after takeoff.

items.question_text: For simultaneous operations between a departure and an arrival on parallel runways, what is the required divergence between the departure course and the missed approach course?
items.explanation: The paragraph requires the departure course to diverge immediately by at least 30 degrees from the missed approach course. This divergence must be maintained until separation is applied, and one of the listed conditions (such as runways separated by at least 2,500 feet) must also be met.

choices.text: At least 30 degrees immediately, maintained until separation is applied.
At least 15 degrees within 1 mile of the runway end.
At least 45 degrees immediately from the runway heading.
Any divergence angle is acceptable as long as the runways are parallel.

items.question_text: During simultaneous approaches using reduced separation, one aircraft executes a go-around. What is the controller's obligation before the reduced separation is lost?
items.explanation: The transitional procedure requires expeditiously issuing control instructions to increase separation between the applicable aircraft before the approved reduced separation is lost. These instructions must establish approved separation through altitude and/or lateral separation via divergence. The controller cannot rely on the published missed approach to automatically resolve the situation.

choices.text: Allow the go-around aircraft to fly the published missed approach — the procedure is designed to maintain separation.
Terminate the approach for the remaining aircraft and have both execute missed approaches.
The reduced separation continues to apply to both aircraft until they are established on new headings.
Expeditiously issue control instructions to establish approved separation before the reduced separation is lost.

items.question_text: A CA alert activates involving an aircraft under your control and another controller's aircraft. The situation requires an immediate turn to prevent a collision. Must you coordinate with the other controller before issuing the turn?
items.explanation: The paragraph states that coordination is not required when immediate action is dictated. Safety of flight takes precedence — issue the necessary immediate control instruction first, then coordinate with the other controller afterward.

choices.text: Yes — but only a brief acknowledgment from the other controller is needed before action.
No — coordination is not required when immediate action is dictated; issue the turn and coordinate afterward.
Yes — all actions must be coordinated with the other controller before any instruction is issued.
No — CA alerts that involve another controller can be ignored since both controllers see the same alert.

items.question_text: A controller's STARS position is about to have terminal automation services terminated for scheduled maintenance. What must the controller do before the termination takes effect?
items.explanation: The paragraph requires informing all appropriate positions before terminating or reinstating use of the terminal automation system at a control position. This ensures all affected controllers are aware of the impending change and can adjust their operations to maintain continuous service.

choices.text: No notification is required — maintenance can proceed at any time.
Only the OS/CIC needs to be notified of the termination.
Inform all appropriate positions before terminating use of the automation system.
Termination occurs automatically at the scheduled time — controller notification is handled by the system.

items.instruction: Determine whether secondary radar may be used as the sole display source.
items.situation: You are working outside Class A airspace. Primary radar is fully operational but a secondary radar system provides better low-altitude coverage in a portion of your airspace where terrain masks the primary radar. There is no primary radar outage — the secondary radar simply sees targets the primary radar misses at low altitude.
items.question_text: Under which of the three conditions for secondary radar as sole display source does this situation fall?

choices.text: The primary radar is temporarily unusable or out of service.
Additional coverage is provided by secondary radar beyond that of the primary radar.
A secondary radar system is the only source of radar data for the area.
None — secondary radar may not be used as a sole source unless primary radar has failed completely.

items.explanation: The three conditions for secondary radar as sole display source outside Class A airspace are: (a) additional coverage beyond primary radar, (b) primary radar temporarily unusable, or (c) secondary radar is the only source. Here, the secondary radar provides additional coverage beyond what primary radar can see due to terrain masking, satisfying condition (a).

items.instruction: Identify all required elements of an EA stop request.
items.question_text: When transmitting a stop request for EA activity, which elements must be included?

choices.text: Facility name only — the military unit will determine the rest from the call.
Facility name and the phrase 'STOP EA ACTIVITY' — no further detail is needed.
Facility name, type of EA activity (stream/burst or buzzer), radar band affected, and expected suspension duration when feasible.
Radar band and expected duration only — facility name is transmitted automatically.

items.explanation: Each stop request must include the facility name, type of EA activity (chaff dispensing as 'stream' or 'burst,' or electronic jamming as 'buzzer'), the radar band affected, and when feasible, the expected duration of suspension.

items.situation: You are providing radar surveillance of an outer fix holding pattern. You observe an aircraft that has drifted outside the depicted holding pattern airspace boundary. The aircraft does not appear to be in immediate conflict with other traffic but is now outside protected airspace.
items.question_text: What should you do?

choices.text: Take no action since there is no immediate traffic conflict.
Issue a Brasher warning for a pilot deviation.
Terminate radar service because the aircraft is no longer in protected airspace.
Assist the aircraft to return to the assigned holding airspace.

items.explanation: The paragraph requires the controller to attempt to detect any aircraft that stray outside the holding area and, upon detecting such an aircraft, assist it to return to the assigned airspace. The absence of immediate conflict does not relieve the controller of this obligation.

items.instruction: Determine whether explicit radar service termination notification is required.
items.situation: An IFR aircraft has landed at a tower-controlled airport where radar coverage does not extend to within 1/2 mile of the runway end. The aircraft has been instructed to contact ground control after clearing the runway.
items.question_text: Must the controller inform the aircraft that radar service is terminated?

choices.text: Yes — arriving aircraft must be informed at tower-controlled airports where radar coverage ends more than 1/2 mile from the runway.
No — the ground control frequency change implies termination.
No — radar service is automatically terminated when any IFR aircraft lands at a tower-controlled airport.
Yes — but only if the aircraft was on a radar approach.

items.explanation: Radar service normally auto-terminates when an aircraft conducting an instrument, visual, or contact approach has landed. However, at tower-controlled airports where radar coverage does not exist to within 1/2 mile of the end of the runway, arriving aircraft must be informed when radar service is terminated — this is an explicit exception to automatic termination.

items.instruction: Determine whether VFR code monitoring may be temporarily suspended.
items.situation: You are working an en route sector that includes Class A airspace only. Numerous VFR Code 1200 targets are visible on your display and they are making it difficult to track IFR traffic.
items.question_text: Do you need to continue monitoring Code 1200?

choices.text: Yes — but only until the excessive targets are identified.
Yes — Code 1200 must always be monitored regardless of airspace class.
No — monitoring of Code 1200 is not required when the area of responsibility includes only Class A airspace.
No — VFR codes may be filtered at any time for any reason.

items.explanation: The paragraph requires monitoring Code 1200, 1202, 1203, 1255, and 1277 'unless your area of responsibility includes only Class A airspace.' Since Class A airspace is IFR-only, VFR codes are not expected or required to be monitored there.

items.instruction: Apply the Mode C validation criteria.
items.situation: You observe an aircraft below FL 180 with a Mode C readout that shows 4,500 feet. The pilot reports being level at 5,000 feet. The altimeter setting has been verified as correct.
items.question_text: Is the Mode C readout valid under the altitude validation criteria?

choices.text: Yes — the difference is within normal instrument tolerances.
Yes — 500 feet is acceptable for aircraft below FL 180.
No — but only because the aircraft is below FL 180.
No — the readout varies by 500 feet from the pilot-reported altitude, exceeding the 300-foot validation threshold.

items.explanation: The paragraph states a Mode C readout is considered valid when it varies less than 300 feet from the pilot-reported altitude. A 500-foot difference exceeds this threshold, making the readout invalid. The controller must not use invalid Mode C altitude information for separation and must follow the procedures for invalid readouts.

items.instruction: Place the following actions in the correct sequence when an inflight VFR deviation request is received from an aircraft with an inoperative transponder.
items.question_text: What is the correct sequence of suggested actions for an aircraft requesting a VFR deviation with an inoperative transponder?

choices.text: Suggest unaffected airspace, then IFR filing, then VFR route with radio contact.
Approve the deviation immediately, then determine routing.
Suggest filing IFR, then suggest VFR route, then suggest unaffected airspace.
Deny the request — aircraft with inoperative transponders cannot receive deviations.

items.explanation: The paragraph specifies the sequence: (1) suggest the aircraft conduct its flight in airspace unaffected by the CFRs, (2) suggest the aircraft file an IFR flight plan, (3) suggest the aircraft provide a VFR route of flight and maintain radio contact with ATC. These are escalating suggestions before a deviation may be approved.

items.situation: You have identified two aircraft using beacon identification. Their targets are now in close proximity on the radar display and their observed maneuvers are similar, creating doubt about which target belongs to which aircraft.
items.question_text: What is the required action?

choices.text: Terminate radar service for both aircraft until identification can be verified.
Use more than one method of identification to resolve the doubt.
Continue with the existing single identification method — proximity alone is not disqualifying.
Ask both pilots to squawk ident simultaneously to distinguish the targets.

items.explanation: The paragraph requires using more than one method of identification when proximity of targets, duplication of observed action, or any other circumstances cause doubt as to target identification. A single method is insufficient when doubt exists.

items.situation: An en route controller initiates an automated point-out to an adjacent sector. Thirty seconds pass with no response from the receiving controller. The aircraft is approaching the sector boundary.
items.question_text: What must the transferring controller do?

choices.text: Assume approval after 30 seconds and permit the aircraft to enter.
Cancel the point-out and vector the aircraft away from the boundary.
Revert to verbal coordination to obtain approval before the aircraft enters the receiving controller's airspace.
Continue waiting — the receiving controller has no time limit to respond.

items.explanation: For en route automated point-outs, if the receiving controller takes no action, the transferring controller must revert to verbal coordination. Silence is not consent — the aircraft may not enter the receiving controller's airspace without approval.

items.instruction: Apply the correct radar separation minimum.
items.situation: You are working a terminal position using a single sensor ASR. An aircraft is 35 miles from the radar antenna. What is the required radar separation minimum?
items.question_text: For a terminal single sensor ASR with the aircraft at 35 miles from the antenna, what separation minimum applies?

choices.text: 1.5 miles.
5 miles.
2.5 miles.
3 miles.

items.explanation: Under terminal single sensor ASR minima, when less than 40 miles from the antenna the minimum is 3 miles. At 35 miles, the aircraft falls under the less-than-40-mile threshold.

items.situation: A controller is sequencing arriving aircraft and issues a speed reduction to 250 knots to one aircraft, then immediately afterward issues a speed increase to 280 knots to the same aircraft because a gap opened ahead of it.
items.question_text: What principle from the speed adjustment application paragraph has the controller violated?

choices.text: Speed adjustments below FL 280 may not exceed 250 knots.
Avoid adjustments requiring alternate decreases and increases.
Speed adjustments must be in increments of at least 20 knots.
Only one speed adjustment per aircraft per sector is permitted.

items.explanation: The paragraph states controllers should avoid adjustments requiring alternate decreases and increases. Constant speed changes in opposite directions are inefficient, increase pilot workload, and may cause confusion. The controller should plan a single appropriate speed rather than oscillating.

items.instruction: Apply PAR course and glidepath transmission frequency requirements.
items.situation: A PAR controller is providing precision approach guidance. The controller issues course and glidepath information and notes the aircraft is slightly above glidepath. How frequently should transmissions occur?
items.question_text: What is the expected transmission interval on a precision final approach?

choices.text: Only when the aircraft deviates from course or glidepath by more than one dot.
Approximately every 5 seconds.
Approximately every 15 seconds.
Approximately every 30 seconds.

items.explanation: The paragraph specifies transmissions on precision final approach should occur approximately every 5 seconds. This is a higher frequency than surveillance approaches (15 seconds) due to the tighter tolerances of precision approach guidance.

items.situation: You need to initiate a coast track for an aircraft with a valid computer-stored flight plan. The note reminds you to perform a start track for the aircraft before handoff. In what mode should the coast track be started?
items.question_text: What mode must be used to initiate the coast track?

choices.text: Coast tracks cannot be initiated for aircraft with stored flight plans.
Free coast tracking mode.
Either FLAT or free — the controller may choose.
Flight Plan Aided Tracking (FLAT) mode.

items.explanation: The paragraph requires initiating coast tracks only in FLAT mode. The exception for 'free' coast tracking applies only to aircraft without corresponding computer-stored flight plan information, as a reminder. For aircraft with a stored flight plan, FLAT mode is mandatory.

items.situation: A CA alert activates on your STARS display for an aircraft under your control. Another controller at an adjacent position is also involved because a second aircraft under their control is part of the conflict prediction.
items.question_text: What is the required initial action and coordination?

choices.text: Evaluate the alert without delay and initiate coordination with the other controller.
Disable the CA function until the alert can be investigated.
Acknowledge the alert and wait for the other controller to initiate coordination.
Immediately issue evasive vectors to your aircraft without coordinating with the other controller.

items.explanation: The paragraph mirrors 5-13-1: when a CA or MCI alert is displayed, the controller must evaluate the reason for the alert without delay and take appropriate action. If another controller is involved, coordination must be initiated to ensure an effective course of action. The 'without delay' language emphasizes urgency.

items.instruction: Prioritize which tracks to suspend when data block overlap creates an unworkable situation.
items.situation: Data block overlap in a holding pattern has created an unworkable display. You need to suspend some tracks. Among the aircraft in the hold, some display automatic altitude readouts and some do not.
items.question_text: Which tracks must be suspended first?

choices.text: Any track — the order of suspension does not matter.
Tracks that are not displaying automatic altitude readouts.
Tracks that are displaying automatic altitude readouts.
Tracks of aircraft with the highest altitudes.

items.explanation: The paragraph specifies a priority order: if necessary to suspend tracks, those which are not displaying automatic altitude readouts must be suspended first. If the condition still exists after suspending those, then tracks displaying automatic altitude readouts may be suspended. This preserves altitude-monitoring capability for as many aircraft as possible.

items.instruction: Determine whether a departing aircraft may be vectored below the minimum IFR altitude.
items.situation: A departing IFR aircraft has just taken off and ISR is not displayed in the full data block. The aircraft has not yet reached the minimum IFR altitude. The controller applies obstacle separation: the flight path is 4 miles from the nearest prominent obstacle depicted on the radar scope and the aircraft is climbing to an altitude 1,500 feet above the obstacle.
items.question_text: May the controller vector the aircraft below the minimum IFR altitude?

choices.text: Yes — the flight path is at least 3 miles from the obstacle and the aircraft is climbing to at least 1,000 feet above it.
Yes — but only if the pilot specifically requests a vector for obstacle clearance.
No — vectors below the minimum IFR altitude are never permitted for departing aircraft.
No — ISR must be displayed in the full data block before any vectors below the minimum altitude may be issued.

items.explanation: The paragraph authorizes vectors below the minimum IFR altitude (when ISR is not displayed) if: the flight path is 3 miles or more from prominent obstacles shown on the radar scope AND the aircraft is climbing to an altitude at least 1,000 feet above the obstacle. Both conditions are met (4 miles and 1,500 feet respectively).

items.instruction: Apply simultaneous independent approach separation requirements.
items.situation: Two aircraft are being vectored for simultaneous independent approaches to parallel runways. During turn-on to the parallel final approach courses, both aircraft are at the same assigned altitude and vertical separation has not yet been established.
items.question_text: What minimum separation must be maintained between the aircraft during turn-on?

choices.text: A minimum of 2 miles radar separation diagonally between successive aircraft.
A minimum of 5 miles radar separation during all turns.
A minimum of 1,000 feet vertical or a minimum of 3 miles radar separation.
No minimum applies during turn-on — separation is only required after both are established on final.

items.explanation: During turn-on to parallel final approach for simultaneous independent approaches, the controller must provide a minimum of 1,000 feet vertical OR a minimum of 3 miles radar separation. Since vertical separation is not yet established, 3 miles radar separation is required. The diagonal separation minimum (1 mile) is for dependent, not independent, approaches.

items.instruction: Identify all circumstances when reported altitudes must be entered into the automation system.
items.question_text: When Mode C altitude information is unavailable or unreliable, in which circumstances must reported altitudes be entered?

choices.text: At every pilot transmission and every 5,000 feet of altitude change.
Only when the aircraft reaches the assigned altitude.
At assigned altitude, on climb/descent clearance, and each 10,000 ft during climb/descent to/from FL 180+.
Only during the initial climb from the departure airport to the cruising altitude.

items.explanation: The paragraph requires entry (a) when the aircraft reaches assigned altitude, (b) when an aircraft at an assigned altitude is issued a clearance to climb or descend, and (c) a minimum of each 10,000 feet during climb to or descent from FL 180 and above. These sustained updates ensure maximum accuracy in applying slant range correction formulas.

items.front: What is the difference between the OS/CIC notification requirement and the adjacent-facility notification requirement for radar malfunctions?
items.back: OS/CIC notification is mandatory for any radar malfunction or unexpected outage. Adjacent facility notification is conditional — required only when appropriate to the situation.

items.front: When may a terminal facility conduct a secondary-radar-only ASR final approach?
items.back: Only when an emergency EXISTS AND the pilot CONCURS. Otherwise, secondary-radar-only ASR final approaches are prohibited for terminal facilities.

items.front: Which three categories of radar-identified aircraft are subject to merging target procedures (when not in a holding pattern)?
items.back: 1. Aircraft at 10,000 feet and above. 2. Turbojet aircraft regardless of altitude. 3. Presidential aircraft regardless of altitude.

items.front: What are the protected airspace dimensions for an RNAV ATS route?
